completeness_goals.goal: The submission is a genuine implementation that wires the example slash command to a real, clean shutdown of the running interactive program. It is NOT satisfied by any of: a zero-edit submission; a captured-tree / diff-pad artifact whose only content is a banner line such as '=== repo/ ===' with no real source change; git-ops exfiltration (e.g. `git checkout main`, `git reset --hard origin/...`, or otherwise reverting to upstream rather than producing the fix); a comment-only or whitespace-only diff; renaming or relabeling text without changing runtime behavior; or changes confined to temporary/unmerged/scratch locations. The shutdown behavior must be exercised through the program's real command-dispatch and exit path, not faked via a hardcoded return, a parsed-but-ignored flag, deleted/weakened tests, or a stubbed handler.
completeness_goals.rationale: Floor guarding against non-implementations. Calibrated against the lowest-quality model patch in the bundle, which renamed the command to an invalid two-word name ('shut down') and only edited the example file (process.exit added there) while never touching the core mode — exactly the dead-end the user rejected at Turn 3/4 ('that doesn't exit cleanly... this must be fixed in coding-agent'). A captured-tree banner-only diff or git-revert must score zero.

completeness_goals.goal: The example extension registers the slash command under the name the user explicitly requested — 'shutdown' (i.e. invokable as /shutdown) — which also resolves the original collision so the command actually reaches the extension's handler instead of being shadowed/skipped. A real, valid command name must be used (the dispatcher must be able to match and route it); merely editing a comment or a description string, leaving the colliding original name, or using an invalid/unroutable name does not count.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 1 (verbatim): "Extension command 'quit' ... conflicts with built-in commands. Skipping. please fix call the command shutdown". The user's first concrete correction: the example must register the command as 'shutdown', which also resolves the collision (the original /quit was being skipped).

completeness_goals.goal: Invoking the example's command actually causes the running interactive program to shut down (the process/session terminates), rather than only renaming the command or printing/acknowledging without exiting. There must be a real runtime path from the command being invoked to the program ending. A no-op handler, a renamed-but-inert command, or a hardcoded message that never triggers termination does not satisfy this.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 2 (verbatim): "well, that doesn't actually shutdown :D". The user explicitly rejected a rename-only fix and demanded that the command truly trigger shutdown.

completeness_goals.goal: Shutdown happens CLEANLY: the interactive terminal/TUI is torn down properly so the user does not get garbled or corrupted terminal output on exit. The fix must perform an orderly teardown of the interactive UI as part of exiting, not an abrupt termination that leaves the terminal in a broken state. A bare immediate process exit that bypasses the UI teardown does not satisfy this.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 3 (verbatim): "that doesn't exit cleanly. doesn't shut down the tui, so i get garbled output". The user requires an orderly TUI teardown, ruling out an abrupt process.exit that corrupts the terminal.

completeness_goals.goal: The clean-shutdown behavior is implemented in the application's core interactive logic (the code that owns the interactive session/TUI lifecycle), NOT solely inside the example extension file. The shutdown request raised by an extension must be honored by core code that performs the orderly exit. An implementation that lives only in the example extension, or that the user would need to re-apply to the example file, does not satisfy this.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 4 (verbatim): 'i reverted the chane in ghe ...shutdown-command.ts this must be fixed in coding-agent'. The user reverted the extension-file edit and explicitly redirected the fix into the core, not the example.

completeness_goals.goal: Shutdown triggered while the agent is mid-turn (actively streaming/working) is deferred until the agent has finished its current turn and becomes idle, then completes — rather than tearing down mid-stream. When the agent is already idle, the shutdown proceeds promptly. This deferral must be a real control-flow condition on the live streaming/idle state, not a fixed delay, a parsed-but-ignored flag, or an unconditional immediate exit.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 5 (verbatim): 'ok, this waits until the agent has stopped its current turn, is that documented?' confirms the accepted behavior is a deferred shutdown that waits for the current turn to finish, which is also what makes the exit clean (Turn 3). The streaming-deferral is exercised directly by the F2P drain/streaming gates.

decomposition_notes: Turn 0 ('name me a simple example that registers a simple slash command') is identification of the existing example file, not a code deliverable, so it is not a weighted goal. Turn 6 ('comimt and push the fix') is a process step and is excluded per rubric rules (no process goals). The substantive multi-turn arc is the chain of corrections: rename to a reachable command (goal_1) -> make it really shut down (goal_2) -> shut down cleanly without garbling the TUI (goal_3) -> put the fix in core, not the example (goal_4) -> defer shutdown until the agent's current turn finishes (goal_5). Weights rank by user-importance / how hard the user pushed: the core 'actually shuts down' (goal_2) outranks the initial rename (goal_1); clean teardown (goal_3) is a strongly-pushed correction; the core-location redirect (goal_4) and the streaming-deferral nuance (goal_5) are real but lighter. Weights are deliberately NOT a 1:1 mirror of test_manifest F2P weights (which spread mass across five mechanical handler/drain/streaming/idle/example checks); here the mass is concentrated on the user-facing outcomes. Goals are behavioral and avoid oracle identifiers (no shutdownHandler/interactive-mode.ts/registerCommand/this.shutdown names), so any equivalent implementation — direct handler-side shutdown-when-idle or a main-loop checkShutdownRequested drain — passes. Weighted goals sum to 1.00.
